data.title: CHI: Attractive Valuation But Not A Buy Yet
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered Calamos Convertible Opportunities and Income Fund (CHI), I issued a hold rating due to the risks associated to its portfolio allocation. The fund allocates a sizeable portion of its assets to debt securities with low credit ratings, which introduces the fund to the risk of defaults. Since my last coverage, CHI has provided attractive total returns that slightly outpaced the S&P 500 Index. Since my last coverage, CHI has released an updated annual report that I wanted to use to reassess the fund's value proposition now that we've crossed into the new year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CHI's share price has declined by about 1.75%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 10.1% over the same time frame. CHI now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 10.1% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. When reviewing the annual report, CHI's portfolio strategy has demonstrated its efficiency at generating earnings that can outpace distributions. Therefore, I have confidence that CHI can sustain its current payouts over the next twelve months, which is ideal for investors seeking income stability.

The interest rate environment has also shifted over the last year, so I believe that CHI may not present as much of a risk profile as it did in the past. Assuming that interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months, CHI may experience improved price growth over time. However, there are some risks that should be considered before imitating a position. For instance, CHI is likely to underperform standard index-tracking ETFs during market declines. Additionally, the fund's current portfolio structure means that CHI is likely to pay out more than it earns if market indices decline and the fund cannot capture sufficient net realized gains.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, CHI has total managed assets of $1.27B that are spread across a diverse range of income producing assets. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 2.16%, but this is a bit misleading since this includes costs related to operating expenses and interest. When excluding these other costs, the actual management fee sits at 0.8% of assets. The fund has a primary goal of generating attractive total returns through a high current income and some capital appreciation.

In order to achieve this goal, the fund will allocate at least 80% of its assets into convertible and non-convertible income-producing securities. Convertible securities operate as a hybrid instrument that allows management to convert one of these positions into common shares if they believe the holding has the potential for capital appreciation. The portfolio's breakdown by investment type is as follows:

  • Convertibles: 69.52%
  • Corporate Bonds: 22.83%
  • Bank Loans: 4.17%
  • Cash & Receivables: 1.72%
  • Common Stock: 0.97%
  • Synthetic Convertibles: 0.61%
  • Asset Backed: 0.06%

Expanding on the fund's assets, CHI diversifies its positions across different sectors. This helps mitigate risks associated to any one specific focus area and can reduce volatility. As we can see below, CHI is most heavily allocated to Information Technology positions at 29.3% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Industrials and Consumer Discretionary securities at 13.3% and 10.5% of assets respectively.

When looking at the positions within the fund, we can see that CHI is directly exposed to some of the highest quality companies in the world. However, you will notice that nearly all of the securities are structured as convertible bonds, rather than common equity. Therefore, CHI may not be able to directly participate in the growth of these positions. For instance, CHI is most heavily allocated to Lumentum Holdings (LITE) at 2.3% of assets. This is followed by other notable names such as the Boeing Company (BA), Uber Technologies (UBER), and Alibaba Group (BABA), just to name a few.

Lastly, the fund utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings when market conditions allow. The use of leverage currently amounts to 34.03% of assets, which is equivalent to $434.4M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage during this high interest rate environment. Since interest rates remain elevated, this means that CHI may be allocating a greater portion of its earnings towards debt maintenance. So if the cost of debt is high, this makes the use of leverage a bit more risky since leverage can also increase downside risks.

So even though the Fed initiated three different 25 basis point cuts to interest rates in 2025, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. Assuming interest rates continue to trend downward over 2026, this may be a positive growth catalyst for the share price. Looking back over the last few years, we can see how lower interest rates helped propel the fund higher throughout 2020 and 2021.

Valuation & Performance Insights

At the time of my last coverage, CHI traded at a slight premium to NAV of 3.2%. Despite the share price slightly increasing since then, CHI now trades at a very small premium to NAV of 0.36%. For reference, CHI has traded at an average premium to NAV of 5.88% over the last five year period. Referring to the red line on the chart below, we can see that CHI's price NAV valuation remains attractive compared to its historical range. While there were definitely some better entry points over the last decade, CHI now sits at its most attractive level since late 2021.

Since my last coverage, CHI also released an updated annual report for 2025. This is the only report that provides transparency into the fund's internal earnings. Net investment income amounted to a loss of $0.14 per share, while net realized gains landed at $2.33 per share. Unfortunately, CHI has struggled to generate consistent net investment income since interest rates were increased throughout 2022. A higher interest rate may be negatively impacting net investment income due to the elevated costs of leverage and small operating spread. Therefore, CHI has remained entirely reliant on positive net realized gains to provide NAV growth and support the distributions that are being paid.

The challenge is that net realized gains can wildly vary since they are influenced by market conditions. Therefore, the fund is dependent on positive market conditions so that CHI can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Likewise, this also means that CHI isn't capable of offering any compelling resistance against market downturns. If the fund experiences net realized losses, this is likely to lead to large declines in its NAV. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $12.54 per share
  • 2021: $15.49 per share
  • 2022: $10.26 per share
  • 2023: $8.99 per share
  • 2024: $10.04 per share
  • 2025: $11.12 per share

Another aspect that is affected by the fund's structure is that CHI isn't likely to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation over time. If the fund is relying on net realized gains, this means that CHI is essentially selling off all its gains to fuel the distributions. Therefore, the fund's positions aren't able to grow uninterrupted over a longer holding period. When looking at the performance over the last decade, we can see that this structure has resulted in CHI's share price only increasing by 30.4%. When including all distributions paid, the total return jumps up to 248.2% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that at least ~85% of their total return will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions.

Additionally, the fund's high dividend yield and portfolio structure can actually lead to larger losses during market declines. Since the fund has no way of generating income that can offset net realized losses, CHI may find itself in a scenario where it is paying out more than it actually earns in income. This can lead to rapid share price erosion with every distribution that is paid. A good example of this was demonstrated throughout 2022's market decline. As we can see below, CHI experienced a total loss of 23.3%, which was greater than the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) over the same time frame. Therefore, the high dividend yield doesn't automatically mean that CHI is a good defensive position during declines.

Variable Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.095 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 10.1%. If we multiply this monthly distribution by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.14 per share. Referring to the same annual report for 2025, we can see that CHI's dividend coverage is a bit variable based on market conditions. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $2.86 per share
  • 2021: $4.05 per share
  • 2022: ($4.10) per share
  • 2023: ($0.13) per share
  • 2024: $2.19 per share
  • 2025: $2.19 per share

As we can see, CHI has demonstrated its ability to generate earnings that can outpace distributions. Although there was weakness during 2022 and 2023, this is understandable given the uncertainty of the pandemic and shifting interest rates at the time. However, I really like the fact that CHI has demonstrated that its portfolio strategy can effectively generate earnings that can support several years worth of payouts in a single period. For instance, 2025's earnings were an indication of this:

2025's earnings of $2.19 per share / $1.14 annual payout = 1.92 years.

Looking forward, I believe that the fund's performance can improve when interest rates trend downward. Lower interest rates can improve CHI's operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities. Therefore, I believe that CHI will be able to sustain its current distribution rate over the next twelve months. This makes CHI a solid choice for investors seeking income stability within their portfolio.

Lastly, investors should consider the potential tax consequences from CHI's distributions. For instance, the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that the majority of distributions were supported by the use of short-term capital gains, which is classified as ordinary dividends and have the greatest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in CHI would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors looking to limit their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am maintaining a hold rating on CHI for now. The fund trades at a compelling price to NAV valuation in relation to its historical range. However, the latest annual report indicates that the fund is entirely reliant on net realized gains to support NAV growth and cover distributions. While this is fine during bull markets, I remain cautious about the fund's resilience if market momentum shifts. During a decline, it is clear that CHI's price may erode over time since the fund will pay out more than it actually earns in income. However, these issues can be improved in a scenario where interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months. I would like to see an improved rate of net investment income going forward, which can create better resilience to flat or declining markets.

Manager Commentary

“Overall, the quarter reflected growing scrutiny of earnings quality and return potential, signaling an early shift toward businesses supported by tangible cash flows, balance sheet strength, and nearer-term earnings visibility.”

Earnest Partners LLC

Market in Review

The U.S. mid-cap equity market delivered modestly positive returns during the fourth quarter of 2025, extending a strong year, marking the third consecutive year of double-digit annual gains. While full-year performance was largely driven by sustained appreciation in areas of the market where valuations reflected long-duration growth expectations rather than near-term earnings visibility, the composition of returns in the fourth quarter differed meaningfully and highlighted an evolution in market leadership.

Through the first three quarters of the year, performance was influenced by enthusiasm around artificial intelligence and related growth themes. In the fourth quarter, however, momentum moderated and returns became more differentiated. Leadership shifted toward sectors with clearer earnings profiles and more durable fundamentals led by Health Care, with Industrials, Information Technology, and Materials.

In contrast, several other sectors experienced more mixed results during the period, including Real Estate, Utilities, Communication Services, Consumer Discretionary, and Energy. Overall, the quarter reflected growing scrutiny of earnings quality and return potential, signaling an early shift toward businesses supported by tangible cash flows, balance sheet strength, and nearer-term earnings visibility.

Portfolio Performance

In the fourth quarter of 2025, the Harbor Mid Cap Core ETF returned 3.82% (NAV), outperforming its benchmark, the Russell Midcap® Index, which returned 0.16%.

Both sector positioning and stock selection contributed positively to relative performance. Stock selection was notably strong within the Materials, Energy, and Industrials sectors, reflecting effective positioning in companies benefiting from improving fundamentals and cash-flow generation. Allocation effects were also additive, notably in the Health Care and Industrials sectors. Importantly, the portfolio achieved these results while maintaining a disciplined focus on businesses with sustainable earnings profiles, valuation support, and strong balance sheets rather than pursuing short-term market leadership.

Contributors and Detractors

Contributing to performance was Murphy Oil Corporation (MUR). Headquartered in Houston, TX, Murphy Oil is an oil and natural gas producer with a balanced mix of onshore and offshore operations, anchored in the U.S. and complemented by an international presence, including in Canada, Africa, and offshore assets in Vietnam. The portfolio is supported by an active exploration and development pipeline that can add longer-term growth. Shares gained 11% in the quarter as the company reported an earnings beat in the third quarter driven by higher production, stronger pricing, and sharply lower lease operating expenses, gathering, processing, and transportation costs.

Adjusted earnings and cash flow exceeded expectations as barrels of oil equivalent per day volumes rose ~15%. Murphy remains well positioned to potentially drive earnings growth given its portfolio of low-cost oil and gas assets in Eagle Ford and the Gulf of Mexico, both of which provide strong margins and cash generation. With non-core asset sales and debt reduction strengthening the balance sheet, and capital spending expected to decline as projects including the Civette well and Vietnam move into production, the company remains well positioned to potentially grow earnings and free cash flow through the forecast period.

Another contributor was Ulta Beauty, Inc. (ULTA), a leading U.S. specialty beauty retailer selling cosmetics, fragrances, haircare, and skincare through its stores and website/app, across both branded and private-label products. Founded in 1990 and headquartered in Bolingbrook, Illinois, Ulta is known for its large loyalty program and broad, one-stop assortment.

ULTA shares gained more than 10% in the quarter after the company reported results ahead of market expectations. Management reported 6.3% same-store sales growth, driven by both increased spending per trip and increased transaction volumes in the period. Management also highlighted that while roughly 80% of revenue is generated by in-store sales, digital revenue remains a growth engine, evidenced through its tracking of digital engagement. The company continues to invest heavily in digital capabilities and personalization, which is expected to help further drive growth and support potential profitability going forward.

Another contributor was Hexcel (HXL). Hexcel shares were flat year to date through September 30, 2025, before rallying approximately 18% in the fourth quarter. As the only vertically integrated U.S. producer of aerospace-grade composite materials, Hexcel is a critical supplier to both commercial and defense aircraft programs and potentially positioned to benefit as commercial aircraft build rates move roughly 25% higher toward pre-pandemic levels.

Results from the first half through the third quarter of 2025 were pressured by temporary customer destocking tied to supply chain disruptions and production resets at Boeing and Airbus, which together account for roughly three-quarters of revenue. However, inventory levels are now normalizing as higher build rates have been announced. As production recovers and supply chain noise fades, Hexcel should see improved demand visibility, improved manufacturing efficiency, and earnings potential that is not yet fully reflected in current market expectations.

Detracting from performance was Raymond James (RJF), a diversified financial services firm that provides wealth management, asset management, and capital markets services, operating primarily through an independent advisor model serving individual and institutional clients. The firm benefits from recurring fee revenue, long-tenured advisor relationships, and a broad platform that supports both personal financial planning and corporate financing needs.

During the quarter, shares declined by 7.0% despite adjusted earnings exceeding consensus expectations by 10%, reflecting investor focus on near-term margin pressure rather than underlying business momentum. Revenue growth remained solid, driven by continued expansion in assets under administration and advisory activity, with Private Client Group revenues rising 7% year over year, Asset Management revenues up 14%, and Capital Markets revenues growing 6%.

The primary headwind was higher operating expenses across compensation, technology, and regulatory costs, which weighed on short-term profitability but reflect investments to support future growth rather than a deterioration in demand. Raymond James remains well positioned given its entrepreneurial advisor model, strong recruiting environment, and ability to capture market share during periods of market uncertainty. Historically, volatile markets have accelerated advisor migration from larger banks to independent platforms, supporting long-term asset, revenue, and earnings growth beyond short-term cost fluctuations.

Another detractor was SBA Communications Corp. (SBAC), whose stock underperformed during the quarter due to a cyclical dip in telecom operator CapEx, as the industry digests healthy CapEx invested during the initial years of the 5G ramp-up. Longer term, growth prospects for SBAC remain healthy, driven by built-in price escalators in SBAC’s tower contracts (~3% annually) and densification of 5G coverage (involves hanging more radios on towers for which SBAC gets additional rent) in the U.S. As initial capacity investments are digested by the exponential growth in data, and expanding 5G coverage internationally, the long-term prospects for SBAC’s towers remain robust. Densification in conjunction with price escalators should drive longer-term secular revenue growth in the mid-to-high single digits. With significant operating leverage in the model (very little incremental cost to SBAC when additional radios are added to SBAC’s towers) driving higher earnings growth, SBAC is an attractive investment at the current valuation.

Buys and Sells

We added EMCOR Group, Inc. (EME), to the portfolio during the quarter. EMCOR provides electrical and mechanical construction, industrial maintenance, and facilities services across the United States and United Kingdom. Its operations span electrical power and distribution systems, HVAC and refrigeration, building operations and maintenance, and industrial services for commercial, industrial, and government customers.

We also added Vertiv Holdings Co. (VRT), which designs, manufactures, and services critical digital infrastructure for data centers, communication networks, and commercial and industrial facilities. Its portfolio includes power and thermal management systems, switchgear, integrated rack solutions, modular infrastructure, and monitoring and control software.

Twilio, Inc. (TWLO), which develops cloud-based communications software and platforms that enable businesses to embed messaging, voice, email, and customer engagement capabilities into their applications, was added to the portfolio during the quarter. Its offerings span core communications APIs, as well as data, engagement, and customer experience solutions.

We sold Air Lease Corp. (AL), a leading aircraft leasing company that specializes in providing a wide range of commercial aircraft to airlines worldwide, helping it expand its fleets and meet its operational needs. We sold the stock because the investment thesis came to fruition.

Sector Underweights and Overweights

The portfolio has overweights to Industrials, Health Care, Information Technology, and Financials. The portfolio is underweight to Utilities, Communication Services, Consumer Staples, Consumer Discretionary, and Energy.

Earnest Partners does not target sector or industry weights. Instead, the weightings are an outgrowth of our bottom-up, fundamental stock selection process. Our process is designed to put the client in the individual securities that we believe have attractive expected returns — relative overweights and underweights are an outgrowth of where we are finding those individual opportunities. The risk management process also influences the weights taken in any one sector. As a general rule, sectors will not represent more than the greater of 35% or 2.5x the benchmark weight.

Outlook

As long-term investors with a three- to five-year horizon and low portfolio turnover, we remain focused on owning high-quality businesses that we believe are best positioned to deliver relative outperformance through full market cycles. While macroeconomic factors continue to evolve, our objective is not to forecast short-term macro outcomes, but rather to ensure — on an ongoing basis — that both existing holdings and prospective investments are positioned to navigate a range of economic environments.

We continually assess companies through our proprietary screening process, fundamental research, and risk framework, with particular emphasis on earnings durability, balance sheet strength, competitive positioning, and valuation. Despite periods of macro-driven market activity, portfolio construction remains guided by bottom-up conviction and risk discipline. The portfolio reflects our highest-conviction ideas at any point in time, structured to support long-term, risk-adjusted outperformance through varying market and economic conditions.

Market price returns are based upon the closing composite market price and do not represent the returns you would receive if you traded shares at other times.

Performance data shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Past performance is net of management fees and expenses and reflects reinvested dividends and distributions. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and when redeemed may be worth more or less than their original cost. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Current performance may be higher or lower and is available through the most recent month end at harborcapital.com or by calling 800-422-1050.

IMPORTANT INFORMATION

Risks

Investing involves risk, principal loss is possible. Unlike mutual funds, ETFs may trade at a premium or discount to their net asset value. Harbor ETFs are new and have limited operating history to judge.

There is no guarantee that the investment objective of the Fund will be achieved. Stock markets are volatile and equity values can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market and economic conditions. Stocks of mid cap companies pose special risks, including possible illiquidity and greater price volatility than stocks of larger, more established companies.

Benchmarks

The Russell Midcap® Index is an unmanaged index generally representative of the U.S. market for medium capitalization stocks. This unmanaged index does not reflect fees and expenses and is not available for direct investment. The Russell Midcap® Index and Russell® are trademarks of Frank Russell Company.

Disclosures

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund's total annual operating expense.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet’s attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

Best and Worst Performers sections reflect stocks in the portfolio for the quarter with an average weight of 0.25% or greater.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

As a result of changing market conditions, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund before investing. To obtain a summary prospectus or prospectus for this and other information, visit harborcapital.com or call 800-422-1050. Read it carefully before investing.

Foreside Fund Services, LLC is the Distributor of the Harbor ETFs.

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

A basis point is one hundredth of 1 percentage point.

data.title: Orion Group Holdings: An Improved Balance Sheet To Drive Growth
data.description:
Summary

I am positive on Orion Group Holdings (ORN). My summarized thesis is that ORN fundamentals have gotten better given the increased focus on data centers and a stronger balance sheet. The opportunity pipeline is also extremely large relative to ORN’s size, which gives good visibility to growth.

Company overview

ORN is a specialty construction company with two segments: Marine and Concrete. The Marine segment provides dredging and construction services for marine transportation facilities, bridges, pipelines, and environmental structures. The main customers for this segment include federal agencies, such as the US Navy and the US Army Corps of Engineers, state transportation departments, and even private port operators. The Concrete segment delivers turnkey concrete construction services, including site preparation, formwork, and pouring, focused on commercial, industrial, and institutional projects.

The Pivot to High-Value Infrastructure and Data Center Exposure

The best part about ORN business is that it has an opportunity pipeline of $18 billion, with >$1 billion of bids pending award. This is a massive deal funnel relative to its revenue base of ~$800 million in FY24. If ORN is to win the >$1 billion bid, it is safe to say the next 1 or 2 years of revenue are basically in the bag. Now, visibility is great, but I think a more important aspect here is the quality of this backlog/deal pipeline. Over the past few years, management has shifted the Concrete segment away from low-margin, highly cyclical light commercial work and toward more complex industrial projects. By that, I mean data centers.

Data centers now account for ~27% of Concrete revenue and ~27% of the total pipeline, based on management’s comments on the Q3 2025 call. Think of the magnitude of opportunity here. ~27% of FY24’s Concrete segment revenue is ~$74 million, but ~27% of the total pipeline is $4.86 billion. Even if ORN just wins 10%, it is $486 million, or ~6.5x FY24’s data center revenue. And I think there is a real shot for ORN to capture demand here because we are still early in the buildout phase of AI-related infrastructure. As much as the headlines typically focus on GPUs and memory, the reality is that this buildout requires real, physical assets. For the data center to be up and standing, someone has to pour the foundations. For those that do not follow the data center industry, a primary constraint today is labor, and ORN, due to the nature of its core business, has a pool of labor that it can readily pivot to capture demand.

The growth outlook is not the only attractive aspect. Because building a data center is much more complex, more specialized, and less price-competitive (hyperscalers care more about time-to-market vs. price) than typical commercial construction, the pricing and margins are better. There are also no problems with cash flow and payment since customers have deep balance sheets (referring to the hyperscalers).

Balance Sheet De-Risking and Asset Monetization

ORN’s equity story is only as good as its balance sheet. Liquidity was the main overhang on this stock for a long time, and you can see it in the balance sheet. Either the leverage ratio was too high (back in FY22/23) or cash was too low (last twelve months' cash is only $4.9 million). Good news for us is that ORN is now past this phase with the sale of the East & West Jones property that brings in $23.5 million. The structure of this sale was also noteworthy because ORN retained the rights to operate it. Under the agreement, ORN secured exclusive rights for ten years to continue delivering dredge spoils to the site. That means ORN keeps the logistical benefit without having its capital tied up in the real estate. I’d say this is perhaps the most optimal outcome in raising liquidity vs. diluting shareholders or taking on more debt.

The other leg to this liquidity story is that on December 23, 2025, ORN signed a new credit agreement totaling $120 million. The facility includes a $60 million revolver, a $20 million equipment loan, and a $40 million term loan. While this new structure cost ORN $1.1 million in make-whole fees to exit the prior agreement, I think the benefits outweigh the cost. The old structure was tight and restrictive because it was held with a non-bank lender (White Oak Commercial Finance) during ORN’s turnaround phase (started in Aug 2022). In that circumstance, the credit line was really more of a "rescue capital" that carries higher interest rates and stricter covenants. This new agreement gives management more room to operate, as it is now signed under normal conditions with a commercial bank.

This should unlock the growth path ahead, as ORN now has sufficient liquidity to bid on large projects.

Valuation

My expectations are that ORN will see topline growth acceleration, mainly driven by the sheer magnitude of data center opportunities. ORN did 12% y/y growth in FY24 when the data center tailwind was less apparent. As the world becomes more reliant on AI, I don’t think 10% growth is a high bar to clear. Accordingly, margins should go up as ORN takes on more complex work. Back in FY20 and FY17, ORN did achieve ~8% and ~11% adj. EBITDA margin, so there are precedents for margins to go higher. Given that ORN is a smaller-scale company compared to larger peers like Construction Partners (ROAD), Everus Construction Group (ECG), etc., it is fair that ORN trades at a discount. These peers currently trade at mid-teens forward EBITDA multiples, and I am assuming ORN will stick to its current multiple of ~9x forward EBITDA. That gives us an enterprise value of ~$631 million. Net debt position should be near to 0 after the $22 million net cash proceeds from the property sale. All in all, my model suggests a share price target of ~$15.80.

Investment Risk

Given that the contracts ORN handle have prices fixed, it is taking the risk that costs will not blow up. If it does, margins could be way lower than initially expected. Customer concentration is also a risk, as 72% of the pipeline is dependent on public sector funding. Finally, bad weather is an inherent risk that could result in halting operations, which would weigh on the earnings performance.

Conclusion

My positive view on ORN is because this is a business with a much stronger balance sheet and has great visibility to its revenue growth. The pivot toward data centers should also support better margins and demand. The asset sale should provide ORN with necessary liquidity to pursue larger projects. If ORN performs as I modeled, I believe we can see quite an attractive upside over the next few years.

data.title: Roche's CT-388 Adds Optionality In The GLP Market, But Wait For Dips
data.description:
Roche Holding AG (RHHBY) presented, via its US subsidiary Genentech, the Phase 2 results for CT-388. This is a once-weekly dual GLP1/GIP receptor agonist, and its trial was conducted in adults with obesity or overweight without type 2 diabetes (T2D). Roche highlighted competitive outcomes of placebo-adjusted weight loss of 22.5% for patients who followed the planned regimen, and placebo-adjusted weight loss of 18.3% for patients who missed doses or discontinued. More interestingly, Roche quickly announced that its Phase 3 program, with two trials, Enith1 (obesity and overweight patients without T2D) and Enith2 (obesity and overweight patients with T2D), is expected to begin this quarter.

Exciting GLP Breakthrough

Roche Holding AG is a well-known pharma giant. However, one of its smaller US subsidiaries is called Genentech. Interestingly, this subsidiary is in charge of developing Roche’s new weight loss candidate. Recently, Genentech released positive topline results for CT-388 in a Phase 2 trial and announced it expected to start Phase 3 studies later this quarter. That Phase 3 will encompass two trials called Enith1 and Enith2. Given this promising breakthrough, I thought it was worthwhile to dive into its implications for Roche’s stock going forward.

First of all, note that Roche also confirmed there is another Phase 2 trial ongoing (CT388-104). However, this is a separate trial that will evaluate CT388-104 in people with obesity and overweight who also have type 2 diabetes (T2D). This matters because Roche’s long-term approach may come from a combination of CT-388 with a complementary mechanism. Concretely, they may combine it with petrelintide, which is developed in Phase 2 in collaboration with Zealand Pharma (ZLDPF).

Having said that, note that Roche is a late entrant to the high-demand obesity drugs based on glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP1) / glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) receptor agonist and next-generation metabolic drugs. Currently, Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY) have had an earlier start. NVO raised the bar in this market with Wegovy (semaglutide), which was authorized by the FDA in 2021 and is a GLP1-based, not dual GLP1/GIP. On the other hand, LLY entered the market later with Zepbound (tirzepatide), which was approved in 2023 for chronic weight management. Tirzepatide is also a GLP1/GIP receptor agonist. But prior to its weight management indication, in 2022, LLY’s tirzepatide was approved for diabetes treatment with the label Mounjaro.

So, in that context, it made sense for Roche to throw its hat into the ring and try to develop its own weight loss asset. By January 2024, Roche bought CT-388 when it purchased Carmot Therapeutics in a $2.7 billion deal. At the time of the acquisition, the drug was ready to start Phase 2, and Roche initiated Phase 2 in August 2024. Later, the company added a complementary mechanism to treat obesity, petrelintide, with a deal with Zealand Pharma.

Moreover, this collaboration with Zealand Pharma aims to co-develop and co-commercialize petrelintide as monotherapy and in combination with CT-388 and is worth up to $5.3 billion. But more importantly, it would allow Roche to tap into a rapidly growing GLP market. In fact, this asset category alone is projected to reach a TAM of $132.8 billion by 2035, showcasing it’s a massive opportunity for Roche.

Interestingly, Roche’s objective here is to build a platform with two mechanisms: incretin (CT-388) plus petrelintide, which is an amylin analogue. Amylin matters because it’s a pancreatic hormone involved in satiety and glucose regulation. Both drugs may be delivered together in one product, and the idea is that the satiety produced by the amylin pathway can be additive or complementary to the incretin-driven (GLP1/GIP) weight loss. Also, this combo may help to improve tolerability and durability. And more importantly, it could theoretically give Roche a competitive advantage in the weight loss market relative to single mechanism drugs (like NVO’s or LLY’s).

CT-388’s Market And Competitiveness

With that in mind, note that CT-388 is essentially a once-weekly injection and is a dual GLP1/GIP receptor agonist. GLP1 (glucagon-like peptide-1) and GIP (glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide) are incretin hormones released by the gut after meals. CT-388 is designed to activate both the GLP1 receptor (GLP1R) and the GIP receptor (GIPR), instead of only GLP1R, like semaglutide. The activation of GLP1R reduces appetite and increases satiety via central nervous system (CNS) pathways. Also, it improves glucose control. The activation of GIPR is thought to enhance weight loss and metabolic effects.

Additionally, Roche’s recent study had a population of 469 adults with obesity (BMI ≥30) or overweight (BMI ≥27 to <30). These patients didn’t have type 2 diabetes (T2D) and were administered up to 24 mg as the highest dose tested, with treatment testing duration of 48 weeks. The primary endpoint was the percentage change in body weight from baseline to week 48. With that setup, CT-388 produced 22.5% placebo-adjusted weight loss in participants who fully followed the treatment plan. And, those who missed doses, discontinued, or in general did not fully follow the plan, showed a placebo-adjusted weight loss of only 18.3%.

Thus, these results intuitively suggest it’s competitive with the results achieved by LLY’s tirzepatide. Plus, since this is a market where differentiation is crucial, Roche says that CT-388 not only activates GLP1 and GIP receptors but also tries to turn them on in a specific way that might stay effective longer, reducing desensitization. This feature may be demonstrated with the result in the Phase 2 trial that showed that weight loss didn’t reach a plateau at week 48.

In my view, if Roche hits that benchmark on patients after 48 weeks, it could be a game-changer in the field. However, note that this is nonetheless a longer-term bet. Roche’s Phase 3 trials, Enith1 (in obesity and overweight patients without T2D), and Enith2 (in obesity and overweight patients with T2D), have an estimated primary completion in August 2028. Therefore, the company will need time to corroborate the efficacy and tolerability of CT-388, or probably at least another 2 years, before any FDA approvals.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, Roche currently trades at a $385.0 billion market cap, so it’s undoubtedly a megacap by now. Its June 2025 financials show its balance sheet holds $15.2 billion in cash and short-term investments, against $41.6 billion in financial debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities). However, Roche is already a cash flow machine. I estimate that in Q2 2025 alone, they generated around $3.4 billion in positive cash flow. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations minus CAPEX.

But that number showcases that the company is more than capable of covering its quarterly interest expenses of $383.2 million, while funding its multiple R&D projects (like CT-388). Also, its book value reached $41.7 billion, implying a slightly expensive P/B of 9.2. For comparison, the sector’s median P/B is much closer to just 3.0, suggesting Roche is already trading at a premium. And indeed, the shares are up around 40% since early November 2025, so it makes sense that its multiples look stretched at these levels.

However, I believe a simple way of assessing Roche’s upside potential related to its new GLP asset is to consider the theoretical incremental revenues this may generate. In that sense, first consider that according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on Roche, the company is projected to generate $83.0 billion in revenues this year. However, as I previously noted, GLP drugs are projected to reach a TAM of $132.8 billion by 2035. But note that the same forecast assumes that by 2029, TAM will be worth $76.5 billion. And remember that by 2028, Roche’s Phase 3 with Enith1 and Enith2 should be ready with its primary endpoint trial data.

Hence, assuming they don’t face major regulatory/clinical setbacks, we could see their GLP asset start contributing with revenues by 2029. Naturally, here lies the speculative portion of the analysis. We can’t know for sure what Roche’s market share will be if it receives approval for its new GLP asset. Nor do we know how quickly it ramps up its sales upon approval. Plus, it’s entirely feasible that this whole R&D program fails and no approvals are ever reached. So, these are substantial uncertainties that should be discounted, and if we see any negative headlines about CT-388, you should expect a sharp pullback for the stock.

Yet, assuming Roche secures around a 5% market share in the GLP space by 2029, that alone would be a $3.8 billion in incremental revenues (based on the market forecast I used before). This would be equivalent to just 4.6% of its 2026 revenues, so it doesn’t seem this alone should justify Roche's 40% surge since last November. But then again, Roche is soon reporting its 2025 full-year results on January 29, 2026. So, I anticipate management will give us a clearer picture of CT-388’s competitiveness and potential market share if approved. And, given the recent positive Phase 2 trial data, I imagine they’ll likely guide to very optimistic figures, so the stock may have room to run post-earnings.

Conclusion: Ideally A Buy On Dips

Overall, I think Roche’s recent spike since November 2025 is probably largely due to its newly added potential in the massive GLP market. However, by now I have to admit the market is indeed pricing in a substantial premium on the shares. That’s why I feel Roche is ideally a “buy on dips” type of stock at these levels. Additionally, there are several assumptions and uncertainties embedded on this new GLP trial results they announced. So, I wouldn’t recommend chasing this recent spike. But if the stock retraces post-earnings (market reactions to earnings are always a gamble), then I feel Roche will probably be a great investment opportunity for long-term investors.

data.title: Allspring Common Stock Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Strategy

Public equity markets are often driven by emotion, requiring successful investors to have conviction in individual securities and diversification across sectors.

Our team's conviction comes from an in-depth private market valuation (PMV, the price an acquirer would pay to purchase the entire company) process of analyzing the business model, competitive positioning, key trends, management, and other proprietary metrics.

We believe that the PMV of a company is much more stable than its associated public market stock price.

Our proprietary PMV investment process provides us with a consistent and accurate discipline to exploit market emotion by buying company stock at a discount to their PMV and selling them as they get fully valued.

Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap may be increased or the commitment to maintain the cap may be terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Quarterly review

  • The Allspring Common Stock Fund outperformed the Russell 2500 Index during the period.
  • Stock selection in industrials and consumer discretionary sectors contributed to relative portfolio performance while selection in information technology (IT) impeded quarterly results.

Market review

Markets closed the year on a strong note, with momentum building in the final months despite ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty. The fourth quarter brought a broad rebound as inflation eased and the Federal Reserve (Fed) shifted to a more supportive stance, cutting rates by 75 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) across three meetings. Investor confidence improved as trade tensions cooled, and leadership rotated toward industrials, semiconductors, and select health care names while consumer staples and discretionary sectors lagged. Artificial intelligence (AI)-related companies remained in focus, though concerns about profitability and leverage tempered enthusiasm late in the quarter. Against this backdrop, small- and mid-cap stocks posted modest gains, with the Russell 2500 Index up 2.22% for the quarter, and active strategies benefited from targeted exposure to industrials and health care.

For the year, equity markets delivered their third consecutive annual advance. The S&P 500 Index rose 18%, the Nasdaq was up 20%, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average added nearly 13%. Gains were concentrated in large-cap technology names, leaving the equal-weight S&P 500 Index trailing by more than 700 bps. AI optimism dominated headlines, while sectors such as managed care, apparel, and commodity chemicals performed poorly. Early-year volatility, when the S&P 500 Index fell nearly 19% amid tariff uncertainty, gave way to recovery as trade concerns eased and monetary policy turned accommodative. Despite these tailwinds, active managers faced challenges, with only 29.7% outperforming the S&P 500 Index, underscoring the difficulty of navigating concentrated leadership and the need for disciplined research. In addition, throughout 2025, the equity market was characterized by a persistent “momentum trade” that often decoupled from fundamental valuations, as the top-performing decile increasingly included companies with high price velocity but negative or inconsistent profitability. This speculative fervor around emerging technologies allowed non-earning companies to outperform high-quality, cash-flow-positive peers, driving market concentration to historic levels and heightening the risk of a sharp technical reversal.

Fund performance and attribution

The Allspring Common Stock Fund outperformed the Russell 2500 Index during the period. Gains were supported by favorable stock selection and sector allocation, with industrials as a key contributor while consumer discretionary also added incremental strength. IT remained a headwind, reflecting ongoing volatility in tech-related areas amid heightened scrutiny of AI-driven business models.

The broader market backdrop was mixed, with resilient consumer spending and strength in select cyclical sectors offset by softness in labor markets and uneven performance across technology segments. Against this environment, the portfolio’s positioning in areas tied to industrial demand and select consumer names provided a measure of stability and relative advantage.

For the full year, the strategy faced a challenging landscape and ultimately lagged its benchmark, as persistent weakness in IT weighed heavily on results. While sector allocation offered a modest offset, negative stock selection across several segments proved difficult to overcome. The year was marked by significant macroeconomic uncertainty, including tariff-driven trade disruptions and shifting sentiment around AI that contributed to pronounced swings in market leadership. Despite these headwinds, the portfolio maintained a disciplined approach, emphasizing diversification and long-term positioning, even as market narratives evolved rapidly throughout the year.

Top Contributors And Detractors To Quarter-End Fund Performance

Contributors

  • Revolve Group, Inc. Class A (RVLV)
  • Haemonetics Corp. (HAE)
  • Kirby Corp. (KEX)
  • Bruker Corp. (BRKR)
  • Allient Inc. (ALNT)

Detractors

  • Chewy, Inc. Class A (CHWY)
  • Teledyne Technologies Inc. (TDY)
  • AAON, Inc. (AAON)
  • National Vision Holdings, Inc. (EYE)
  • CCC Intelligent Solutions Holdings Inc. (CCC)

The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

Stocks Contributing To Relative Performance Include:

Revolve Group, Inc. (RVLV), operates an online fashion platform offering apparel and accessories primarily to millennial and Gen Z consumers, generating revenue through direct-to-consumer sales. In the fourth quarter, Revolve’s stock rose as its revenue met expectations with a 4.4% year-over-year increase. Gross margins outperformed, surpassing forecasts by 100 bps compared with last year and 150 bps above market estimates, due to tighter promotions and effective inventory management. Despite concerns about potential margin pressures from tariffs, these fears have not materialized, and RVLV has continued to gain market share.

Haemonetics Corp. (HAE) provides blood management solutions and related technologies to health care providers, generating revenue through systems and services that support plasma collection, hospital procedures, and blood center operations. In fiscal second quarter 2026, Haemonetics delivered strong performance, raising guidance and posting margin gains that signal operational discipline. The company’s differentiated portfolio—spanning plasma technologies and hospital solutions—positions it well to capture share in high-growth segments. Haemonetics outperformed the benchmark this quarter, supported by broad-based execution and favorable mix shifts.

Recent catalysts include momentum in plasma and hospital businesses alongside benefits from portfolio realignment. Momentum in the plasma segment could be fueled by a stretched low-income consumer. The hospital segment has gone through a leadership transition that reset numbers lower. Our belief is they are now positioned to grow at a high-single-digit percentage as we look out 12 months.

Bruker Corp. (BRKR) develops and supplies scientific instruments and analytical solutions for research and industrial applications, generating revenue through product sales and related services. BRKR delivered strong performance in the quarter, outpacing peers with a robust earnings beat, and raised forward guidance that bolstered investor confidence. Growth was driven by strong demand for scientific instruments and life science tools as research activity stabilized. Strategic investments in innovation and new markets, combined with operating leverage, favorable product mix, and effective cost control, supported better-than-expected margins. Easing concerns around China exposure and macroeconomic uncertainty, along with improved sentiment toward life science tools, contributed to multiple expansion and relative stock outperformance versus the benchmark.

Stocks Detracting From Relative Performance Include:

Chewy, Inc., Class A (CHWY) operates an e-commerce platform focused on pet products and services, generating revenue through direct-to-consumer sales and subscription programs. CHWY shares declined in September after the company reported elevated selling, general, and administrative expenses. Even though third quarter results released in December were positive—with customer growth rebounding (+5% as net ads are improving sequentially), total revenue growth of +8.3% (implying continued market share gains), and margin expansion (gross margin up 50 bps year over year and adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization margin up 100 bps)—the stock stayed in a trading range. Notably, management highlighted expectations of stronger operating leverage in fiscal-year 2026, which we see as an important factor for the stock's outlook next year.

AAON, Inc. (AAON), is a leader in highly configurable HVAC solutions for commercial, industrial, and data center environments, emphasizing efficiency and performance. In the quarter, AAON produced strong revenue growth driven by data center customers, very strong data center orders, strong orders for traditional HVAC equipment, and improved operational performance after recent missteps. Despite that, the stock performed poorly after initial enthusiasm around its earnings report due to lingering enterprise resource planning (ERP)-driven operational concerns and worries around fluid competitive conditions in the data center HVAC market. AAON brings unique customized thermal management solutions for customers in the fast-growing data center market, as validated by extreme orders growth and backlog expansion over the past year. In the traditional rooftop business, the company is poised to see a growth inflection in 2026 as they lap cyclical pressures and operational disruptions that affected its ability to ship product in 2025. Both businesses should see material revenue growth in 2026+, which, combined with the completion of recent capacity expansions and improved ERP implementation performance, should drive a positive inflection in earnings growth.

National Vision Holdings, Inc. (EYE), operates optical retail stores and provides eye exams and eyewear products, generating revenue through a mix of managed-care partnerships and direct consumer sales. In the fourth quarter, National Vision faced a more challenging backdrop as pricing tailwinds moderated and operational complexity weighed on sentiment. The company’s scale and ability to serve value-conscious customers remain key differentiators in a competitive category. National Vision underperformed the benchmark this quarter in spite of strong fundamentals, including accelerating comparison-store sales. The stock was pressured by a 2026 outlook that reflects more surgical price-taking, which has been a key driver over the past year of the positive inflection in the business. The goodness that drove the stock through much of 2025 may be more difficult to come by in the coming year as we anniversary the fourth quarter and first quarter pricing they took.

Fund positioning and outlook

We expect markets to remain sensitive to macroeconomic developments, including the trajectory of Fed policy and ongoing trade negotiations. While consensus points to further rate cuts and easing inflation, uncertainty around tariff structures and regulatory shifts could sustain volatility. At the same time, secular growth drivers, particularly in IT and industrial innovation, continue to offer compelling opportunities for active managers. With market leadership likely to broaden beyond mega-cap names, we believe a disciplined focus on fundamentals and selective exposure to companies with strong balance sheets and pricing power should be critical to delivering long-term value.

As we enter 2026, we remain steadfast in our commitment to valuation discipline and broad-based diversification, ensuring that our portfolios are not overexposed to any one theme or factor. By focusing on durable businesses and maintaining a selective, nimble approach, we believe we are well positioned to navigate potential volatility and capture the long-term growth opportunities that emerge when the market inevitably returns to a broader focus on value.

data.title: Armanino Foods May Experience Margin Pressures Soon
data.description:
With a new management team led by CEO Deanna Jurgens, the specialty Italian food manufacturer Armanino Foods (AMNF) is now planning to expand beyond high-end grocery stores by selling its pesto to fast-food restaurants. Armanino already has a sizable customer base in the restaurant industry, so management is confident in this plan even though the restaurant industry has been experiencing headwinds recently. The company is also planning to invest in more equipment so it can sustain its rapid growth.

Armanino reported great Q3 2025 results, with revenue rising 11.2% to $19.65 million and net income rising 16.3% to $4.77 million. The company’s gross profit also rose 22.9% to $99.3 million, and its gross profit margin rose 470 basis points to 49.5%. So, it appears that Armanino has strong pricing power in comparison to other CPG companies right now and is also improving its efficiency. This is a great result at a time when other CPG companies’ margins are falling.

It also looks like this company isn’t a hidden gem for value investors anymore. Its stock price has risen a lot in the past year, and now it’s trading at a similar valuation to other CPG companies. I still think that Armanino can continue growing, but I also think its margins are likely to come under pressure in 2026 for several reasons. One of them is that it might be hard for this company to maintain high margins while marketing its sauces to major fast-food chains. So, I think Armanino’s margins are likely to weaken and think it would be better to wait for a pullback before buying this stock.

Armanino Will Need to Justify Its Prices to Fast-Food Restaurants

In Armanino’s January 2026 investor presentation, the company explained that it’s offering restaurants convenience and flexibility. Quick-service restaurants won’t have to prepare their own sauces if they buy them from Armanino, so it will be easier for them to add new limited-time menu items without making their cooks do additional work. The new sauces will add exciting new tastes and help the restaurants satisfy their customers and justify their menu prices. Additionally, Armanino considers itself a premium brand in comparison to other companies that sell sauces to restaurants.

If I was pitching The Marzetti Company (MZTI) to investors, I’d make similar arguments. The Marzetti Company has recently been performing well because it’s selling sauces to value-oriented fast-food chains that are also performing well in the current economy. So, fast-food restaurants are interested in buying sauces right now. But the Marzetti Company is also larger than Armanino, so it has additional scale advantages. And The Marzetti Company has a TTM gross margin of 24%, which is less than half of Armanino’s gross margin. This tells me that Armanino is not competing on price with The Marzetti Company.

So, I’d say that Armanino does have a market opportunity in quick-serve restaurants. But it must also convince restaurants to pay higher prices for its sauces at a time when restaurants are trying to compete on price and save money anywhere they can. Armanino’s Q3 2025 results show that it was able to convince its current customers in the restaurant industry that it was worth it to pay more for high-quality sauces. Now, it’s planning to make a similar pitch to larger restaurant chains. These restaurants also have strong bargaining power and may be able to force Armanino to reduce its margins.

Armanino’s Grocery Business Can Still Grow a Lot

While Armanino’s management team seems to be focused on opportunities in the QSR sector, this company could gain additional market share in the grocery sector as well. Its investor presentation shows that its products are on the shelves at subsidiaries of Kroger (KR) and Albertsons (ACI). But Armanino’s products are only available in specific regions of the US right now. So, expanded distribution is another potential growth driver for Armanino. The company already has partnerships with national distributors.

Expanded distribution, by itself, could be a significant revenue growth driver for Armanino. This company has very high margins for a CPG company, which is why it has a TTM P/S ratio of 5.15. It’s also a small company with much lower revenue than other publicly traded CPG companies. It looks like this company was previously a specialty pesto and Italian food producer that sold products to upscale grocery stores and restaurants, not a volume producer. But now, the big grocery store chains and discount stores are also stocking its products, which means it might have a larger market in 2026.

Private-Label Competition Is a Risk for Armanino

I’d say one of the main risks for Armanino is the introduction of private-label sauces by grocery stores. One of the reasons why CPG companies have been limiting their price increases recently is because grocery stores have been stocking more private-label products. Grocery stores are trying to maintain their competitiveness as well, and many of them have been posting weaker sales growth recently.

Additionally, grocery stores aren’t just competing on price with their private-label products anymore. Health-food stores often create high-end private-label products. And major chains like Kroger and Albertsons would also be capable of developing their own premium, private-label pesto if Armanino’s products rapidly gained popularity.

Additional Risks for Armanino

This is an OTC stock that was previously trading on the pink sheets. Additionally, some of its recent financial results aren’t available. And earnings call transcripts aren’t available for this company either. So, while Armanino now has a $368 million market cap and Wall Street analyst coverage, there are risks here that don’t apply to other CPG stocks. Additionally, this is a low volume stock as well. So, it might be hard to exit from a position in this company if its growth plan doesn’t succeed.

Comparative Valuation for Armanino

Armanino’s new management team has provided results for the first 3 quarters of 2025, but some of this company’s financial results aren’t available right now. So, I can’t use the EV/EBITDA ratio here, and I’ll use the forward P/E ratio instead. According to Seeking Alpha, Armanino had a forward P/E of 21 after the market close on January 26. I’d compare this company to The Marzetti Company and McCormick (MKC), which also make sauces. On the same date, The Marzetti Company had a forward P/E of 24, and McCormick had a forward P/E of 20.

So, Armanino doesn’t appear to have much of a growth premium in comparison to other CPG companies that make sauces. Its forward yield of 1.69% is relatively low for a CPG stock, but that’s not as important for a growth stock. And again, this company has a high gross margin in comparison to its peers.

[object HTMLElement]

Estimating Armanino’s Value

Armanino didn’t provide full-year guidance for 2025, although the new CEO did say that this would be a record-breaking year in her January shareholder letter. Going by the results I’ve seen other CPG companies report recently, I’d expect lower growth in the fourth quarter. If I project that Armanino will report $19.1 million in revenue in Q4 2025, the company would report full-year revenue of $75.7 million. With a 24% profit margin, it would report 4Q income of $4.58 million and full-year income of $18.14 million.

Armanino has multiple growth drivers available in 2026, including sales to major fast-food chains and the potential for increased distribution. But I also think the company’s margins could drop. In the earnings report, the company said that it plans to invest in its business next year, and it may need to add more capacity to continue growing. In the 3Q earnings report, the CEO said, “We're reinvesting our gains into automation, process improvements, and talent to build the foundation for sustainable scale. While these initiatives, paired with ongoing macro pressures, may create near-term cost pressure, they are critical to positioning Armanino for long-term success.” So, I’ll project that the company’s revenue will rise 9% in 2026, but its net profit margin will fall from 24% to 23%. That would result in full-year revenue of $81.8 million and net income of $19.77 million.

With a 2026 P/E of 21, Armanino’s market cap would be $415 million. Armanino is also buying back stock, so I’d project that its share count will drop to 31.6 million shares in 2026 as well. That gives me a one-year price target of $13.13 per share. Armanino’s closing price was $11.87 on January 27, so my price target is 11% higher than the current price.

Several SA analysts recommended this stock at much lower prices. While it still has a buy rating, this company’s stock price has run up a lot recently. And the SA Quant score now says it’s a hold. While it still looks like Armanino’s price could rise further, it no longer looks like as much of a deal as it was earlier this year.

Conclusion

Armanino reported great quarterly results and is on track to post great results this year. I think it’s likely to continue growing rapidly in 2026 as well, even if its margins decline a bit. And right now, that seems like a real risk. Armanino’s CEO warned investors that the company expects short-term cost pressures that are likely to reduce its margins. Other CPG companies have also been reporting much weaker margins recently. Meanwhile, Armanino’s stock price has risen 54% in the last year, and it’s no longer trading at a large discount to other CPG stocks. So, I say Armanino is a hold.
